_meta.authoringNotes: Content cross-verified against 2017 ACC/AHA guideline, 2018 ESC/ESH guideline, JNC 7 report, WHO global hypertension estimate 2023, CDC NHANES 2021-2023 data, and UpToDate Lexicomp.

h1Title: Hypertension (High Blood Pressure): Symptoms, Causes, and Treatment
heroOverview: Hypertension — persistently elevated blood pressure in the arteries — is the world's leading preventable cause of death, quietly damaging blood vessels, the heart, brain, and kidneys for years before symptoms emerge. Defined as a systolic reading of 130 mm Hg or higher, or a diastolic reading of 80 mm Hg or higher by the 2017 ACC/AHA guideline, it affects roughly 47% of US adults and an estimated 1.28 billion people worldwide (WHO 2023). Because it is largely asymptomatic until catastrophic events occur, it is called the 'silent killer.' Effective treatment — lifestyle change first, then medications — reduces the risk of stroke by 35-40%, heart attack by 20-25%, and heart failure by 50%. This guide covers how blood pressure works, what drives it upward, how it is diagnosed and staged, and what evidence-based treatment looks like at every stage.

keyStats.prevalence: 47% of US adults have hypertension (ACC/AHA 2017; CDC NHANES 2021-2023); ~1.28 billion people affected globally (WHO 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Prevalence rises with age — roughly 70-80% of adults over 65 have hypertension; higher rates in non-Hispanic Black adults (56%) vs non-Hispanic White (48%) and Hispanic adults (44%)
keyStats.avgAge: Average age of onset in the US is the mid-40s for men, early 50s for women; rises sharply after 55 in both sexes
keyStats.globalCases: ~1.28 billion people worldwide; 41% of men and 31% of women have hypertension globally (WHO Global Health Observatory 2023)

definition: Hypertension (ICD-10: R03.0; I10 for essential/primary) is a chronic elevation of arterial blood pressure that imposes sustained mechanical stress on the vascular wall, driving progressive damage to the heart, brain, kidneys, eyes, and systemic arteries. Blood pressure is the product of cardiac output and systemic vascular resistance, and hypertension results from chronic increases in one or both of these variables. The 2017 ACC/AHA guideline defines elevated BP as systolic 120-129 mm Hg with diastolic <80 mm Hg; stage 1 hypertension as systolic 130-139 or diastolic 80-89 mm Hg; and stage 2 hypertension as systolic >=140 or diastolic >=90 mm Hg. A hypertensive crisis (systolic >=180 or diastolic >=120) is a medical emergency requiring immediate evaluation for end-organ damage. Hypertension is broadly classified as primary (essential, 90-95% of cases) with no single identifiable cause but well-characterized risk factors including salt intake, obesity, inactivity, alcohol, genetics, and stress — or secondary (5-10% of cases) caused by an identifiable condition such as primary aldosteronism, renal artery stenosis, pheochromocytoma, obstructive sleep apnea, Cushing's syndrome, coarctation of the aorta, or medication effects. The condition is managed primarily by cardiologists, primary care physicians, and nephrologists depending on complexity and complications.

typesClassification.description: Accounts for 90-95% of cases. No single identifiable cause; results from a complex interaction of genetic predisposition (50-60% heritability), high sodium intake, low potassium, obesity, insulin resistance, physical inactivity, chronic stress, alcohol excess, and ageing. Blood pressure rises gradually over years, typically beginning in the 40s in men and post-menopause in women.

Elevated systolic BP (>=130) with normal diastolic (<80). Most common in adults over 65 and caused by age-related arterial stiffening — the aorta and large arteries lose elasticity, so systolic pressure rises while diastolic may fall (wide pulse pressure). Carries high stroke and cardiovascular risk.

Blood pressure elevated in clinical settings but normal at home or on ambulatory monitoring. Affects roughly 15-30% of people diagnosed with hypertension. Associated with increased cardiovascular risk compared with truly normotensive individuals, warranting lifestyle intervention and close monitoring.

Normal blood pressure in clinic but elevated outside clinical settings. Affects roughly 10-15% of the general population. Associated with worse outcomes than white-coat hypertension because it often reflects underlying arterial stiffness and metabolic risk. Requires ambulatory or home monitoring to detect.

Blood pressure that remains above target (>=130/80) despite concurrent use of three antihypertensive agents of different classes, ideally including a diuretic. Affects roughly 10-15% of treated hypertensives. Requires secondary-cause workup (primary aldosteronism, renal artery stenosis, sleep apnea, CKD, pheochromocytoma) and consideration of fourth-line therapies.

Urgency: BP >=180/120 without acute end-organ damage — lower oral medications over 24-48 hours. Emergency: BP >=180/120 with acute end-organ damage (encephalopathy, stroke, MI, pulmonary oedema, aortic dissection, eclampsia) — requires immediate intravenous therapy in an ICU.

primarySymptoms: Most people with hypertension have no symptoms whatsoever — which is why it is called the silent killer. Symptoms do not appear until blood pressure reaches severe or crisis levels.
Severe headache, usually described as pulsatile or thunderclap, occurring at the back of the head — a hallmark of hypertensive crisis and posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES).
Shortness of breath, particularly during exertion or when lying flat (orthopnoea), suggesting cardiac strain or pulmonary congestion from severely elevated BP.
Visual disturbances, including blurred vision, double vision, or transient visual loss — may indicate hypertensive retinopathy, retinal haemorrhage, or an impending cerebrovascular event.
Nosebleeds (epistaxis) that are heavy or difficult to stop — elevated BP increases pressure in the fragile nasal mucosa vasculature.
Dizziness or lightheadedness, particularly with positional changes — can reflect either severely elevated BP or over-treatment with antihypertensives.
Chest pain or tightness on exertion (angina) — the heart muscle is working against excessive afterload, reducing coronary perfusion.
Palpitations or an awareness of irregular heartbeat — may accompany hypertensive urgency or indicate arrhythmia triggered by longstanding pressure overload.
Facial flushing — a sensation of warmth and redness in the face can accompany sudden BP elevation, though it is nonspecific.
Fatigue and exercise intolerance — the heart is chronically overworked and systemic perfusion may be compromised in advanced disease.
earlyWarningSigns: A systolic reading of 120-129 mm Hg with normal diastolic — 'elevated BP' — which progresses to hypertension in most adults within 4 years without intervention
A family history of hypertension in both parents approximately doubles personal risk
Black race is an independent risk factor for earlier onset, more severe hypertension, and greater end-organ damage
Habitual high sodium intake (>2,000 mg/day) — most Americans consume 3,400 mg/day
Overweight or obese BMI (>=25), particularly visceral adiposity, which drives sympathetic activation and RAAS upregulation
Chronic insomnia or sleep deprivation — each hour of sleep lost is associated with roughly 0.5 mm Hg rise in BP the following day
emergencySigns: Blood pressure >=180/120 mm Hg with any of the following symptoms: severe headache, confusion, chest pain, shortness of breath, visual changes, focal neurological deficits — possible hypertensive emergency
Sudden focal neurological weakness, slurred speech, facial droop, or vision loss — stroke (ischaemic or haemorrhagic) requires immediate emergency care
Severe chest pressure or crushing chest pain radiating to the jaw, left arm, or back — possible myocardial infarction
Acute shortness of breath with pink frothy sputum — flash pulmonary oedema from acute left-ventricular failure
Severe headache, altered mental status, seizures — posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) or hypertensive encephalopathy
Sudden severe back or flank pain with haematuria — possible aortic dissection or renal infarction
Pregnant patient with BP >=160/110 and headache, visual changes, or RUQ pain — possible eclampsia, a life-threatening obstetric emergency

causes.description: The dominant dietary driver of primary hypertension in salt-sensitive individuals. High sodium intake expands extracellular volume, increases cardiac output, and directly promotes vascular smooth muscle contraction via sodium-calcium exchange. Approximately 50-60% of hypertensives are salt-sensitive. Reducing sodium from 3,400 mg to under 2,300 mg daily lowers systolic BP by 2-8 mm Hg.

The key hormonal regulator of blood pressure. Renin cleaves angiotensinogen to angiotensin I, which ACE converts to angiotensin II — a potent vasoconstrictor that also stimulates aldosterone release, causing sodium and water retention. In some hypertensive patients, the RAAS is inappropriately activated at normal sodium intake, driving chronic vasoconstriction and volume expansion.

Chronic stress, obesity, sleep apnea, and insulin resistance all drive sympathetic tone upward, increasing heart rate, cardiac contractility, and peripheral vasoconstriction. Elevated sympathetic activity is present in roughly 50% of essential hypertensives and is the mechanism behind many medication-induced BP elevations (e.g., decongestants, stimulants, caffeine excess).

With age and atherosclerosis, large elastic arteries (especially the aorta) lose compliance. The stiffened aorta cannot buffer systolic pressure waves, raising systolic BP, while reduced elastic recoil fails to maintain diastolic pressure — producing isolated systolic hypertension and a wide pulse pressure. This is the dominant mechanism in hypertension in adults over 65.

The vascular endothelium normally produces vasodilators (nitric oxide, prostacyclin) and vasoconstrictors (endothelin) in balance. In hypertension, oxidative stress, inflammation, and insulin resistance impair nitric oxide bioavailability, shifting the balance toward vasoconstriction and perpetuating the elevated pressure state.

Visceral adiposity secretes leptin and adiponectin, activates the RAAS, and promotes systemic inflammation — all raising blood pressure. Insulin resistance independently impairs vasodilation and promotes sodium retention. Roughly 75% of hypertensive patients have metabolic syndrome traits.

Recurrent nocturnal hypoxia activates the sympathetic nervous system, causes oxidative stress, and triggers RAAS activation. Approximately 50-60% of resistant hypertensives have obstructive sleep apnea. Treatment with CPAP reduces BP by 2-5 mm Hg in compliant patients.

The kidneys regulate sodium and extracellular volume; when renal perfusion falls or kidney tissue is damaged, the RAAS activates and BP rises. CKD is both a cause and a consequence of hypertension. By GFR category G3a (eGFR 45-59), hypertension is present in over 80% of patients.

riskFactors.description: Arterial stiffness increases with age; most adults develop some degree of hypertension after 55. Systolic BP rises steadily after the fourth decade while diastolic plateaus and may fall after the sixth decade.

Heritability is estimated at 50-60% from twin and family studies. First-degree relatives of hypertensive parents have a 2-3 fold increased risk. Multiple genes (including AGT, REN, CYP11B2, ADD1, GNAS) contribute.
Men have higher BP than women before menopause. Women's risk catches up and surpasses men after menopause as the protective effect of oestrogen's vasodilatory and RAAS-modulating effects wanes.

Black adults develop hypertension earlier, have higher average BP, more resistant hypertension, and greater end-organ damage (stroke, CKD, LVH) at diagnosis. Salt sensitivity is more prevalent, and RAAS responses differ.
The average American consumes 3,400 mg sodium daily. Reducing to under 2,300 mg (or 1,500 mg for stage-2 hypertensives) lowers systolic BP by 2-8 mm Hg.
Each 1 kg/m2 increase in BMI raises systolic BP by roughly 1 mm Hg. Weight loss of 10 kg reduces systolic BP by 5-20 mm Hg. Visceral fat is more predictive than BMI alone.

Sedentary adults have a 20-50% higher risk of developing hypertension. Regular aerobic exercise (150 min/week moderate intensity) lowers systolic BP by 4-9 mm Hg — comparable to a single antihypertensive drug.
More than 1-2 standard drinks/day raises BP chronically. Alcohol is responsible for roughly 10% of hypertension cases. Reducing or eliminating alcohol can lower systolic BP by 2-4 mm Hg.
Potassium has a natriuretic effect and vasodilates. The DASH diet, rich in fruits, vegetables, and low-fat dairy, provides 4,700 mg/day potassium and lowers systolic BP by 8-14 mm Hg.
Sustained stress activates the sympathetic nervous system and RAAS, raising BP. Mindfulness-based stress reduction, meditation, and regular exercise all lower BP measurably.
A major contributor to resistant hypertension. Diagnosis requires sleep study; CPAP therapy reduces BP in compliant patients, with greatest benefit in severe OSA.
Reduced GFR impairs sodium excretion and activates the RAAS bidirectionally. Hypertension is present in >80% of CKD patients; controlling BP slows CKD progression.

affectedDemographics: Global: ~1.28 billion people (WHO 2023); 41% of men and 31% of women aged 30-79 worldwide
US: ~116 million adults (CDC 2023); age-adjusted prevalence of 47% in adults; 45% of US adults have uncontrolled BP (NHANES 2021-2023)
Black adults: highest US prevalence (56%), earlier onset, more resistant hypertension, higher stroke and CKD rates at diagnosis
Hispanic adults: moderate prevalence (~44%); higher rates of unawareness and lack of treatment compared to white adults
South Asian and East Asian populations: lower average BP than Western populations but rising with urbanisation, sodium intake, and obesity
Rural low-income populations globally: often higher prevalence due to limited access to fresh food, healthcare, and antihypertensives
diagnosisOverview: Hypertension is diagnosed by measuring blood pressure correctly — the most common source of diagnostic error in clinical practice. The correct method: patient seated with feet flat, back supported, arm at heart level, after 5 minutes of quiet rest, no caffeine or exercise in the preceding 30 minutes, and an appropriately sized cuff (bladder width >=40% of arm circumference). Two or more elevated readings on two or more separate occasions confirm the diagnosis. White-coat hypertension and masked hypertension require out-of-office measurement — either home BP monitoring (HBPM) using a validated device twice daily for 7 days, or 24-hour ambulatory BP monitoring (ABPM), which is the reference standard. ABPM is indicated when there is suspected white-coat or masked hypertension, orthostatic hypotension, episodic hypertension (pheochromocytoma), or refractory hypertension. Once hypertension is confirmed, the workup assesses cardiovascular risk (using 10-year ASCVD risk calculator), screens for secondary causes (renal function, electrolytes, urinalysis, fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipid panel, TSH, urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio), evaluates end-organ damage (12-lead ECG for left ventricular hypertrophy, fundoscopy for retinopathy), and stratifies treatment intensity accordingly.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line screening and diagnosis. Uses an auscultatory (mercury or calibrated aneroid) or validated oscillometric device. Correct technique is essential — errors in cuff size, arm position, patient posture, and measurement timing are common.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Three readings taken 1-2 minutes apart, after 5 minutes of rest. Average of the second and third readings is recorded. Report both systolic and diastolic numbers, arm used, cuff size, and patient position.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the diagnosis when white-coat or masked hypertension is suspected. More predictive of cardiovascular events than office readings. Avoids the white-coat effect and captures BP variability throughout the day.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Use a validated upper-arm oscillometric device. Measure twice daily (morning before medications and evening before dinner), sitting after 5 minutes rest. Record for 7 consecutive days; discard day 1 and average the remaining readings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reference standard for diagnosing hypertension, white-coat hypertension, and masked hypertension. Assesses nocturnal dipping status (loss of normal 10-20% nocturnal BP fall is an independent risk factor).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A portable cuff worn for 24 hours during normal activity. Takes readings every 15-30 minutes during the day and every 30-60 minutes at night. Sleep disruption is common but usually mild.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH), a direct consequence of sustained pressure overload and a major independent cardiovascular risk marker. Also detects arrhythmias, prior silent MI, and conduction abnormalities.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-invasive, takes 5-10 minutes. Electrodes are placed on the limbs and chest. No pain. Readings should be taken before starting antihypertensives if possible, as some drugs (e.g., digoxin) affect ECG findings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: More sensitive than ECG for detecting LVH, diastolic dysfunction, and reduced ejection fraction. Indicated when ECG is abnormal, when heart failure is suspected, or to refine cardiovascular risk in high-risk patients.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-invasive ultrasound of the heart. Takes 30-45 minutes. Gel is applied to the chest and a probe moved over the heart wall. No radiation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Chronic kidney disease is both a cause and consequence of hypertension. eGFR below 60 mL/min/1.73m2 significantly elevates cardiovascular risk and influences drug selection (thiazide diuretic efficacy falls in advanced CKD).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results typically return within hours. Monitor at least annually in hypertensives.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Microalbuminuria is an early marker of hypertensive nephropathy and independently predicts cardiovascular events. Persistent UACR >30 mg/g warrants intensified treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A random spot urine sample. No special preparation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Hypertension and dyslipidaemia share risk pathways and together dramatically amplify cardiovascular risk. LDL cholesterol level directly informs the need for statins and the intensity of BP treatment targets.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 12-hour fast before blood draw. Total cholesterol, LDL, HDL, and triglycerides measured.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Diabetes and hypertension frequently coexist and synergistically damage the vasculature. Insulin resistance is also implicated in primary hypertension. Diabetes shifts the BP target to <130/80 and influences drug choice (ACE inhibitors and ARBs are preferred).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; HbA1c does not require fasting.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Both overt hypothyroidism and subclinical hypothyroidism raise diastolic BP. Hyperthyroidism raises systolic BP. Thyroid dysfunction is a reversible cause of secondary hypertension.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; no fasting required.

diagnosticTests.test: Plasma renin and aldosterone (with aldosterone-to-renin ratio)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screening test for primary aldosteronism, which causes 5-10% of hypertension and is the most common curable form. A positive screen (ARR >30 with aldosterone >10 ng/dL) warrants confirmatory testing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw, ideally in the morning, after the patient has been ambulatory and diet has been unrestricted for 2 weeks. Certain antihypertensives (beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors, ARBs) must be withheld beforehand — coordinate with the ordering clinician.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive screen for renal artery stenosis — a potentially reversible cause of hypertension, particularly in patients with resistant hypertension, rapid-onset hypertension, or flash pulmonary oedema.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Ultrasound of the kidney arteries, takes 30-60 minutes. May require fasting.

treatmentOverview: Treatment follows a risk-stratified, stepped approach. All patients with confirmed hypertension should begin lifestyle modifications concurrently with or before drug therapy: sodium restriction (<2,300 mg/day, ideally <1,500 mg/day), DASH diet, weight loss, regular aerobic exercise (150 minutes/week), moderation of alcohol (<=2 drinks/day men, <=1/day women), and smoking cessation. For stage 1 hypertension (130-139/80-89) without elevated cardiovascular risk (10-year ASCVD <10%), lifestyle alone for 3-6 months is first-line; if BP remains elevated, initiate drug therapy. For stage 1 with ASCVD risk >=10%, or any stage 2 hypertension (>=140/90), initiate lifestyle modification and drug therapy simultaneously. First-line drug classes are thiazide diuretics (chlorthalidone preferred over hydrochlorothiazide based on outcome data), ACE inhibitors or ARBs (preferred in diabetes, CKD, heart failure, or post-MI), and calcium channel blockers (amlodipine preferred, particularly in Black patients). Combination therapy (two drugs from different classes) is recommended for stage 2 hypertension or stage 1 with high cardiovascular risk. The target BP for most adults is <130/80 mm Hg (2017 ACC/AHA); for older adults (>=65) with frailty, orthostatic hypotension, or multiple comorbidities, a target of <140/90 may be appropriate. Treatment intensification should follow a track-to-target principle: if BP is not at goal after 4-8 weeks on monotherapy, add a second agent from a different class; if not at goal on two drugs, investigate adherence and secondary causes; if still uncontrolled on three drugs including a diuretic, the diagnosis is resistant hypertension.

medicalTreatments.name: Thiazide diuretics (chlorthalidone 12.5-25 mg daily; hydrochlorothiazide 25-50 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line antihypertensive for most patients without specific indications for other classes. Work by increasing sodium and water excretion, reducing plasma volume and cardiac output. Chlorthalidone is preferred over HCTZ — a 2022 meta-analysis (Jung et al., JAMA Intern Med) found chlorthalidone reduced cardiovascular events 27% more than HCTZ. Caution in gout (can raise uric acid), hyponatraemia, hypokalaemia, and hypercalcaemia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces systolic BP by 7-10 mm Hg as monotherapy; greater in combination therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: ACE inhibitors (lisinopril 10-40 mg daily; enalapril 5-20 mg twice daily; ramipril 2.5-10 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Block the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone-mediated volume retention. Preferred first-line in diabetes, CKD with proteinuria, heart failure, and post-myocardial infarction. Contraindicated in pregnancy, bilateral renal artery stenosis (can precipitate acute kidney injury), and angioedema. Monitor potassium and creatinine 1-2 weeks after initiation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces systolic BP by 8-14 mm Hg; reduces stroke, MI, and cardiovascular mortality by 20-25% in high-risk patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs) (losartan 50-100 mg daily; valsartan 80-320 mg daily; telmisartan 40-80 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Block the angiotensin-II type-1 receptor, achieving similar hemodynamic effects to ACE inhibitors with lower cough risk (ACE-inhibitor cough occurs in 5-20% of patients). Preferred if ACE-inhibitor cough develops. Contraindications identical to ACE inhibitors.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces systolic BP by 8-14 mm Hg; comparable cardiovascular protection to ACE inhibitors.

medicalTreatments.name: Calcium channel blockers — dihydropyridine (amlodipine 5-10 mg daily; nifedipine extended-release 30-90 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Block L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, causing vasodilation. Highly effective, particularly in older adults, Black patients (where BP is often volume-sensitive and low-renin), and isolated systolic hypertension. Amlodipine is the most prescribed antihypertensive globally. Causes peripheral oedema (via arteriolar dilation without corresponding venodilation) — often mitigated by combining with an ACE inhibitor or ARB.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces systolic BP by 8-12 mm Hg; particularly effective for isolated systolic hypertension.

medicalTreatments.name: Calcium channel blockers — non-dihydropyridine (verapamil 120-360 mg daily in divided or extended-release; diltiazem 120-360 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Also block cardiac L-type calcium channels, reducing heart rate and contractility. Useful in patients with concurrent atrial fibrillation (rate control) or angina. Avoid in heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (negative inotropy).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces systolic BP by 8-12 mm Hg; adds heart-rate control in arrhythmias.

medicalTreatments.name: Beta-blockers (atenolol 25-100 mg daily; metoprolol succinate 50-200 mg daily; carvedilol 12.5-50 mg twice daily; bisoprolol 2.5-10 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Block beta-adrenergic receptors, reducing heart rate and cardiac output. No longer recommended as first-line for uncomplicated hypertension (SPRINT trial showed no benefit over other agents), but remain indicated for hypertension with coronary artery disease, heart failure with reduced EF (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol), post-MI, atrial fibrillation, or migraine. Avoid abrupt discontinuation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces systolic BP by 8-12 mm Hg; mortality benefit in specific indications (HF, post-MI).

medicalTreatments.name: Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (spironolactone 25-100 mg daily; eplerenone 50-100 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Potassium-sparing diuretics that block aldosterone's sodium-retaining and pro-fibrotic effects. First-line add-on for resistant hypertension (the PATHWAY-2 trial showed spironolactone was the most effective fourth-line agent). Preferred over thiazides when serum potassium is low or when aldosterone excess is documented. Monitor potassium closely.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces systolic BP by 12-20 mm Hg as add-on therapy in resistant hypertension.

medicalTreatments.name: Direct vasodilators (hydralazine 25-100 mg twice to thrice daily; minoxidil 2.5-20 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Direct arterial smooth muscle relaxants used in refractory hypertension, hypertensive crisis (hydralazine), or when other agents are contraindicated. Minoxidil is reserved for severe, refractory cases due to significant fluid retention (requires concurrent loop diuretic) and hirsutism. Hydralazine is used in pregnancy (pre-eclampsia).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Hydralazine: 8-12 mm Hg systolic reduction; minoxidil: 15-30 mm Hg systolic reduction — most potent oral vasodilator available.

medicalTreatments.name: Central alpha-2 agonists (clonidine 0.1-0.3 mg twice daily; methyldopa 250-1,000 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Methyldopa is the antihypertensive of choice in pregnancy (used in pre-eclampsia and gestational hypertension; no foetal harm in decades of use). Clonidine is used for refractory hypertension or in patients who cannot tolerate other agents. Both cause sedation and dry mouth. Clonidine should not be stopped abruptly — rebound hypertension can occur.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clonidine: 7-13 mm Hg systolic reduction; methyldopa: 6-8 mm Hg systolic reduction in pregnancy.

medicalTreatments.name: Loop diuretics (furosemide 20-80 mg daily; bumetanide 0.5-2 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Used when hypertension coexists with fluid overload (heart failure, CKD stage 4-5 with oedema). Thiazides lose efficacy at eGFR below 30 mL/min; loop diuretics remain effective. Twice-daily dosing often needed for BP control. Monitor electrolytes and renal function.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Variable; effective for BP control when volume overload is present or eGFR <30.

surgicalOptions.name: Renal denervation (catheter-based)
surgicalOptions.description: An interventional procedure using radiofrequency or ultrasound to ablate sympathetic nerve fibres in the renal arteries. Targets resistant hypertension by interrupting overactive sympathetic signalling from the kidneys. The SPYRAL HTN-OFF MED trial showed clinically meaningful BP reductions without concurrent antihypertensives; the SPYRAL HTN-ON MED trial showed add-on benefit. Not yet standard of care or FDA-approved as of 2026 but available at selected centres for appropriate patients with resistant hypertension.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 24-hour ambulatory systolic BP reduction of 6-10 mm Hg in randomised trials; variable individual response.

surgicalOptions.name: Carotid baroreceptor stimulation
surgicalOptions.description: An implantable device that electrically stimulates the carotid baroreceptors, signalling the brain to reduce sympathetic outflow and lower BP. Available in Europe and under study in the US for resistant hypertension. Reserved for highly selected patients who have failed all medical options.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Systolic BP reduction of 20-30 mm Hg reported in early feasibility studies; invasive and costly.

alternativeTreatments.name: DASH diet
alternativeTreatments.description: Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension — rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, low-fat dairy, lean protein, and nuts; low in saturated fat and sodium. Reduces systolic BP by 8-14 mm Hg in hypertensives. One of the most evidence-backed lifestyle interventions available.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary sodium reduction to <2,300 mg/day
alternativeTreatments.description: Reducing sodium intake by 1,000 mg/day lowers systolic BP by 2-8 mm Hg. The American Heart Association recommends <1,500 mg/day for optimal reduction. Most benefit comes from processed foods (bread, cured meats, canned soups, restaurant meals) rather than table salt.

alternativeTreatments.name: Potassium supplementation (2,500-4,700 mg/day from food)
alternativeTreatments.description: Rich dietary potassium (bananas, potatoes, spinach, beans, avocados) has a modest but measurable natriuretic and vasodilatory effect. Recommended as part of the DASH diet. Potassium supplements should only be used with clinician guidance in patients with CKD or on potassium-sparing drugs.

alternativeTreatments.name: Hibiscus (sour tea / Hibiscus sabdariffa)
alternativeTreatments.description: Multiple RCTs have shown hibiscus tea lowers systolic BP by 7-15 mm Hg, comparable to some antihypertensive medications. Proposed mechanism involves ACE inhibition and vasodilation. Evidence grade: moderate; not a substitute for proven therapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Garlic extract (allicin, aged garlic)
alternativeTreatments.description: Several RCTs and meta-analyses show aged garlic extract reduces systolic BP by 8-10 mm Hg in hypertensives, with greater effect at higher doses (>=1.2 mg allicin per day). Proposed mechanisms include ACE inhibition, antioxidant, and NO-promoting effects. Evidence grade: low-moderate; safe as adjunct.

alternativeTreatments.name: CoQ10 supplementation (100-200 mg/day)
alternativeTreatments.description: Coenzyme Q10 is involved in mitochondrial ATP production and may improve endothelial function. Small RCTs show modest systolic BP reduction (4-17 mm Hg). No major adverse effects. Evidence grade: low; used adjunctively by some cardiologists.

whySeeSpecialist: A cardiologist should be involved when hypertension is resistant to three or more drugs, when secondary hypertension is suspected, when ECG shows LVH or arrhythmia, when there is concurrent heart failure or coronary artery disease, when BP targets are difficult to achieve in older adults with frailty, or when hypertension is diagnosed in pregnancy. Nephrologists manage hypertension complicating advanced CKD or requiring complex diuretic regimens. Endocrinologists evaluate secondary causes including primary aldosteronism, pheochromocytoma, Cushing's, and acromegaly.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board certification in cardiology, internal medicine, or nephrology with demonstrated hypertension expertise. Cardiologists with hypertension or lipid subspecialties are well-suited. Ask about 10-year ASCVD risk calculation at the first visit, out-of-office BP monitoring confirmation, and a clear plan for both lifestyle and pharmacotherapy. Continuity of care is essential — hypertension management is a decades-long relationship, not a single visit.
preventionStrategies: Maintain BMI between 18.5-24.9 — each 1 kg/m2 reduction lowers systolic BP by roughly 1 mm Hg and reduces hypertension incidence by 20% per 5 kg weight loss
Reduce sodium intake to under 2,300 mg/day — most processed and restaurant foods contain far more than people realise; cooking at home and reading labels are the most effective strategies
Engage in regular aerobic exercise for at least 150 minutes per week — brisk walking, cycling, swimming, or equivalent; this alone reduces BP by 4-9 mm Hg
Limit alcohol to <=2 standard drinks per day for men and <=1 per day for women — alcohol raises BP chronically and accounts for roughly 10% of hypertension cases
Consume a DASH-style diet rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and low-fat dairy — this eating pattern lowers BP by 8-14 mm Hg in hypertensives
Quit smoking — smoking raises acute BP and is the dominant modifiable cardiovascular risk amplifier alongside hypertension
Treat obstructive sleep apnea if present — CPAP reduces BP by 2-5 mm Hg in adherent patients and improves nocturnal BP dipping
lifestyleModifications: Adopt the DASH diet — proven to lower BP by 8-14 mm Hg in hypertensives and 2-4 mm Hg in pre-hypertensives; prioritise potassium-rich foods (sweet potato, spinach, banana, beans, yoghurt)
Walk at least 30 minutes on most days — low-to-moderate intensity aerobic activity is as effective for BP as high-intensity exercise and has better adherence
Limit processed food consumption — processed foods account for roughly 70% of dietary sodium in Western diets; cooking fresh meals is the single most effective dietary change
Manage stress with evidence-based approaches — mindfulness meditation (10 minutes daily reduces systolic BP by 4-7 mm Hg per meta-analysis), yoga, or structured relaxation
Achieve 7-9 hours of sleep per night — short sleep duration (<6 hours) is associated with a 20% increased risk of developing hypertension
Monitor home BP regularly — self-measured BP monitoring is the most accurate predictor of long-term cardiovascular outcomes and improves medication adherence

recommended: DASH diet eating pattern — fruits (berries, citrus, banana), vegetables (leafy greens, broccoli, tomatoes), whole grains (oats, brown rice, quinoa), and low-fat dairy (skim milk, Greek yoghurt)
Potassium-rich foods — sweet potato, white potato, spinach, beans, lentils, avocado, banana, tomato paste — target 4,700 mg/day
Lean protein — skinless poultry, fish (especially oily fish 2x/week for omega-3s), plant proteins (tofu, tempeh, legumes)
Nuts and seeds — unsalted almonds, walnuts, flaxseed — provide magnesium and healthy fats
Unsalted seasonings — garlic, lemon juice, herbs (basil, rosemary, thyme), spices (turmeric, cumin) — flavoured food reduces reliance on salt
Water — adequate hydration supports renal function and sodium balance; aim for 1.5-2 litres/day unless restricted for other reasons
avoid: Processed and cured meats — bacon, sausage, hot dogs, deli meats, ham — these can contain 400-1,100 mg sodium per 100g serving
Restaurant and take-away food — most restaurant dishes contain 1,000-3,000 mg sodium per serving; request no added salt
Bread and rolls — a leading hidden sodium source in Western diets; choose low-sodium varieties
Canned soups and prepared sauces — tomato sauce, soy sauce, salad dressings; check labels for sodium content
Salt added at the table — remove salt shakers from the dining table; most people add salt out of habit, not necessity
Excessive alcohol — more than 2 drinks/day (men) or 1 drink/day (women) raises BP chronically and adds calories

exerciseGuidelines: Aerobic exercise is the most evidence-backed form of exercise for hypertension. 150 minutes per week of moderate-intensity activity (brisk walking, cycling, swimming at 50-70% of maximum heart rate) reduces systolic BP by 4-9 mm Hg. Isotonic exercise (walking, cycling) is preferred over isometric exercise (weightlifting) for active BP lowering, though light-to-moderate resistance training is safe and complements aerobic training. High-intensity interval training (HIIT) can lower BP further but is not recommended for uncontrolled stage-2 hypertension until BP is controlled. Breathing exercises (slow diaphragmatic breathing, 6 breaths per minute for 15 minutes daily) lower BP by 3-5 mm Hg by enhancing parasympathetic activity. Stretching improves arterial compliance. The goal is movement most days, ideally daily.
dailyManagement: Take antihypertensive medications at the same time daily — consistency is essential; missing doses causes BP variability which itself increases cardiovascular risk
Measure BP at home using a validated device twice daily (morning before medications and evening before dinner) after 5 minutes seated rest — record readings and bring the log to appointments
Limit dietary sodium — check food labels, avoid processed foods, and minimise restaurant meals; aim for under 2,300 mg/day
Stay physically active — even a 30-minute walk lowers BP within hours; prolonged sitting raises it
Maintain a healthy weight — every kilogram lost reduces BP by approximately 1 mm Hg systolic; even 3-5% weight loss has meaningful impact
Avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) unless essential — they raise BP by 3-6 mm Hg and reduce antihypertensive efficacy by antagonising vasodilation
Avoid grapefruit juice if on calcium channel blockers (amlodipine, nifedipine) — it inhibits CYP3A4 and can dangerously raise drug levels
Limit caffeine before BP measurements — caffeine raises BP acutely for 30-90 minutes; abstain for at least 30 minutes before measuring
prognosis: Untreated hypertension follows a well-documented trajectory of progressive vascular damage. Within 5-10 years, end-organ complications emerge: left ventricular hypertrophy progresses to systolic or diastolic heart failure; carotid and coronary atherosclerosis cause TIAs, stroke, and myocardial infarction; proteinuric nephropathy advances to stage-5 CKD requiring dialysis. The landmark SPRINT trial demonstrated that treating to a systolic target of <120 mm Hg in high-risk adults reduced major cardiovascular events by 25% and all-cause mortality by 27% compared with a target of <140 mm Hg. With effective BP control to <130/80 mm Hg, life expectancy is comparable to that of normotensive individuals. The key variable is not the BP reading itself — it is the number of years the elevated BP went undetected or inadequately treated. Early detection and consistent long-term control are the two decisive prognostic factors.
recoveryTimeline: Lifestyle modifications produce measurable BP reductions within 2-4 weeks for sodium restriction and exercise; weight loss effects accumulate over months. Most antihypertensive drugs reach their full effect within 2-4 weeks of dose optimisation. When initiating or switching therapy, it takes 4-8 weeks to assess whether a regimen is achieving target. Patients with resistant hypertension may require 3-6 months of escalating therapy and specialist review to achieve control. When secondary hypertension (e.g., aldosteronism, renal artery stenosis, sleep apnea) is successfully treated, BP often normalises significantly or entirely — the timeline varies from weeks (post-renal artery revascularisation) to months (after significant weight loss or OSA treatment with CPAP).
complications: Stroke (ischaemic and haemorrhagic) — hypertension is the single most important modifiable risk factor; it doubles the risk of ischaemic stroke and triples the risk of intracerebral haemorrhage; each 10 mm Hg rise in systolic BP increases stroke risk by 30%

Myocardial infarction and coronary artery disease — chronic pressure overload causes concentric LVH, which outgrows its blood supply, causing subendocardial ischaemia; hypertension is responsible for roughly 25-30% of coronary events

Heart failure — both HFrEF (from multiple MIs due to CAD) and HFpEF (from chronic LVH and stiffening) are complications of longstanding hypertension; HFpEF now accounts for over 50% of new heart failure diagnoses

Chronic kidney disease — hypertensive nephrosclerosis is the second leading cause of end-stage renal disease after diabetes; each 5 mm Hg rise in BP accelerates CKD progression; controlling BP to <130/80 slows progression significantly

Hypertensive retinopathy and vision loss — grades III-IV retinopathy (flame haemorrhages, cotton-wool spots, papilloedema) indicate widespread microvascular damage and carry high risk of vision loss and stroke

Peripheral arterial disease — hypertension accelerates atherosclerosis throughout the systemic circulation, including the lower-extremity arteries; PAD affects 12-20% of hypertensives and doubles cardiovascular mortality
Aortic aneurysm and dissection — chronic shear stress on the aortic wall promotes aneurysm formation and dissection; a systolic BP >160 mm Hg multiplies aortic dissection risk approximately fourfold

Cognitive decline and dementia — midlife hypertension is the strongest modifiable risk factor for vascular dementia and a significant contributor to Alzheimer's pathology; controlling BP in midlife reduces dementia risk by roughly 15%

supportResources.name: American Heart Association — Blood Pressure
supportResources.description: The most authoritative US resource for understanding blood pressure numbers, lifestyle guidance, and the latest guideline recommendations.

supportResources.name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention — High Blood Pressure
supportResources.description: Public health resources, statistics, and patient education materials from the CDC.

supportResources.name: WHO Global Hypertension Estimates 2023
supportResources.description: Global prevalence data, policy guidance, and the WHO HEARTS technical package for hypertension management in primary care.

supportResources.name: American College of Cardiology/AHA 2017 Hypertension Guideline
supportResources.description: The full-text guideline defining new BP categories and treatment targets; a foundational document for clinicians and patients.

confusedWithConditions.name: White-Coat Hypertension
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: White-coat hypertension is elevated blood pressure only in clinical settings, with normal readings outside the clinic. It does not represent true sustained hypertension but signals increased cardiovascular risk and warrants lifestyle intervention and periodic monitoring. Ambulatory BP monitoring or home BP monitoring confirms the diagnosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Orthostatic Hypotension
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Orthostatic hypotension is a drop in BP upon standing, causing dizziness or syncope — the opposite physiological problem. It can coexist with hypertension (particularly in older adults on multiple medications), but the two conditions are clinically distinct. One is elevated resting BP; the other is a failure of BP to maintain itself against gravity.

confusedWithConditions.name: Stroke
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Stroke can cause acute BP elevation as a stress response (particularly in haemorrhagic stroke), but the stroke is the acute neurological event — hypertension is usually the pre-existing cause. Hypertension both causes stroke and can result from it (post-stroke BP elevation). The relationship is bidirectional; the distinction matters for post-stroke BP management targets.

confusedWithConditions.name: Heart Failure
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Heart failure can cause secondary hypertension through neurohormonal activation (RAAS, sympathetic), particularly in diastolic dysfunction. However, the BP pattern differs — systolic heart failure typically presents with initially elevated BP declining as the heart fails. Hypertension is more often the cause than the consequence; both conditions worsen each other and frequently coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Atrial Fibrillation
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: AF can cause marked BP variability and is a direct consequence of hypertensive heart disease. However, BP measurement in AF is less reliable and may be falsely elevated or low depending on the arrhythmia. AF itself does not cause sustained hypertension but requires BP monitoring as part of stroke-risk assessment (CHA2DS2-VASc score).

relatedConditions.name: Heart Failure
relatedConditions.relevance: Hypertension is the leading cause of heart failure, accounting for roughly 40% of cases. Chronic pressure overload causes LVH, diastolic dysfunction, and eventually systolic or preserved-EF failure. Treating hypertension prevents 40-50% of heart failure events.

relatedConditions.name: Stroke
relatedConditions.relevance: Hypertension is the most powerful modifiable risk factor for both ischaemic and haemorrhagic stroke, responsible for roughly 50% of attributable risk. Tight BP control (systolic <130) reduces stroke risk by 30-40%.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Kidney Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Bidirectional relationship — hypertension causes arteriolosclerosis in the kidney, reducing GFR; reduced renal function activates the RAAS, raising BP. CKD stage 3+ is present in roughly 25% of hypertensives.

relatedConditions.name: Atrial Fibrillation
relatedConditions.relevance: Hypertension causes atrial enlargement and fibrosis, the structural substrate for AF. Hypertension accounts for roughly 20% of AF cases and doubles the risk. BP control in AF patients reduces AF burden and stroke risk.

relatedConditions.name: Metabolic Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Insulin resistance, visceral obesity, dyslipidaemia, and hypertension cluster together as metabolic syndrome. Each condition amplifies the cardiovascular risk of the others; comprehensive lifestyle intervention benefits all components.

relatedConditions.name: Peripheral Arterial Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Accelerated atherosclerosis in the lower extremities due to chronic hypertension. PAD is present in 12-20% of hypertensives and doubles cardiovascular mortality. BP control is essential in PAD patients.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 Diabetes
relatedConditions.relevance: Roughly 75% of type-2 diabetics have hypertension. Insulin resistance contributes to hypertension; hyperglycaemia accelerates arterial damage. Tight BP control (<130/80) in diabetics reduces microvascular and macrovascular events by 25%.

faqs.question: What is the difference between systolic and diastolic blood pressure?
faqs.answer: Blood pressure is expressed as two numbers: systolic (the top number) measures the pressure in your arteries when the heart muscle contracts and pumps blood out — the peak pressure with each heartbeat. Diastolic (the bottom number) measures the pressure remaining in the arteries when the heart relaxes between beats — the resting pressure. Both numbers matter, but for adults over 50, the systolic reading is the more powerful predictor of cardiovascular risk. Isolated systolic hypertension (high systolic, normal diastolic) is the most common form of hypertension in older adults and is caused by arterial stiffening.

faqs.question: At what blood pressure reading should I be concerned?
faqs.answer: Per the 2017 ACC/AHA guideline: normal is below 120/80 mm Hg; elevated is systolic 120-129 with diastolic below 80; stage 1 hypertension is systolic 130-139 or diastolic 80-89; and stage 2 hypertension is systolic >=140 or diastolic >=90. A reading of 180/120 or higher at any time is a hypertensive crisis requiring immediate medical attention. One elevated reading does not confirm hypertension — it requires at least two elevated readings on two separate occasions, or confirmation by home or ambulatory monitoring.

faqs.question: Can hypertension be cured?
faqs.answer: In most cases — particularly primary (essential) hypertension — there is no permanent cure. However, it can be fully controlled with lifestyle change and, when needed, medication. For secondary hypertension caused by a specific, treatable condition (such as primary aldosteronism, renal artery stenosis, obstructive sleep apnea, or certain medications), curing or treating the underlying cause may normalise blood pressure entirely. For the 90-95% of patients with primary hypertension, treatment is lifelong, similar to the management of hypothyroidism or high cholesterol.

faqs.question: How can I lower my blood pressure without medication?
faqs.answer: Lifestyle modifications can lower systolic blood pressure by 4-20 mm Hg depending on how many changes are adopted and how severely elevated the BP is. The most effective interventions are: weight loss (each 10 kg lost lowers BP by 5-20 mm Hg); the DASH diet (8-14 mm Hg reduction); sodium reduction to under 2,300 mg/day (2-8 mm Hg); aerobic exercise for 150 minutes per week (4-9 mm Hg); limiting alcohol to <=2 drinks/day (2-4 mm Hg); and smoking cessation. Combining all of these can reduce systolic BP by 20-30 mm Hg — comparable to taking two antihypertensive drugs. The key is consistency; benefits accrue over weeks to months.

faqs.question: Does stress cause high blood pressure?
faqs.answer: Acute stress causes a temporary spike in blood pressure through sympathetic nervous system activation — this is normal and not harmful. However, chronic psychological stress leads to sustained sympathetic activation, which contributes to permanent blood pressure elevation. People under chronic stress are more likely to engage in other harmful behaviours (poor diet, alcohol excess, inactivity, poor sleep) that compound the effect. Mindfulness-based stress reduction, meditation, regular physical activity, adequate sleep (7-9 hours), and addressing work-life stressors are all associated with clinically meaningful BP reductions.

faqs.question: Is hypertension hereditary?
faqs.answer: Yes. Heritability estimates from twin and family studies range from 50-60%, meaning genetics account for roughly half of the variation in blood pressure in a population. Multiple genes are involved — variants in the AGT (angiotensinogen), REN (renin), ACE, CYP11B2 (aldosterone synthase), and ADD1 (adductin) genes have been associated with hypertension susceptibility. A first-degree relative with hypertension roughly doubles your personal risk. However, lifestyle factors determine whether a genetic predisposition actually manifests — this is why hypertension prevalence varies dramatically across populations with different dietary and activity patterns.

faqs.question: What happens if I stop taking my blood pressure medication?
faqs.answer: Stopping antihypertensive medication causes blood pressure to return to pre-treatment levels within days to weeks, depending on the drug's half-life (some drugs like amlodipine persist longer than others). This rebound elevation carries immediate risk — the SPRINT trial data show that every mm Hg of untreated elevation compounds cardiovascular risk over time. There is no 'drug holiday' for hypertension management. If side effects are problematic, the solution is to discuss dose adjustment or switching medications with your clinician — not to stop taking them. Abruptly stopping certain drugs (particularly clonidine and beta-blockers) can cause dangerous rebound hypertension.

faqs.question: Does caffeine raise blood pressure?
faqs.answer: Caffeine causes a short-term acute rise in blood pressure of roughly 5-10 mm Hg that peaks 30-90 minutes after consumption and resolves within 3-4 hours. In non-habitual drinkers, this effect is more pronounced. In regular coffee drinkers, tolerance develops within 1-2 weeks and the acute pressor effect diminishes substantially. Long-term coffee consumption is not associated with increased hypertension risk in most epidemiological studies. However, patients with uncontrolled stage-2 hypertension may benefit from limiting caffeine, especially before blood pressure measurements.

faqs.question: Why is high blood pressure called the 'silent killer'?
faqs.answer: Because it causes no symptoms in the vast majority of people until catastrophic damage has already occurred. The first sign of uncontrolled hypertension may be a stroke, a heart attack, sudden vision loss from retinal haemorrhage, or acute kidney failure requiring dialysis. By the time symptoms appear, years of silent vascular damage have already occurred in the brain, heart, kidneys, and eyes. This is why hypertension screening — measuring blood pressure at every clinical encounter from age 18 onward — is so critical. Detecting and treating elevated BP years before symptoms emerge is the key to preventing its complications.

faqs.question: Can I measure my own blood pressure at home?
faqs.answer: Yes — home blood pressure monitoring is strongly recommended by all major guidelines and is more predictive of cardiovascular outcomes than clinic measurements alone. Use a validated upper-arm digital monitor (check validated device lists at stridedbp.org or checkout.heart.org). The cuff size must be appropriate — if the bladder is too small for your arm, readings will be falsely high. Measure twice daily (morning before medications and evening before dinner), after 5 minutes seated rest, with feet flat and arm at heart level. Record readings for 7 consecutive days, discard the first day's readings, and average the rest to share with your clinician.

faqs.question: What is resistant hypertension and what can be done about it?
faqs.answer: Resistant hypertension is defined as blood pressure that remains above target (>=130/80) despite concurrent use of three antihypertensive agents of different classes, one of which should be a diuretic. Roughly 10-15% of treated hypertensives meet this definition. Common causes include: uncontrolled secondary causes (primary aldosteronism, renal artery stenosis, OSA, CKD); excessive sodium intake despite diuretic therapy; medication interactions (NSAIDs, decongestants, stimulants, oral contraceptives); alcohol excess; and white-coat effect. After ruling out these factors, a fourth-line agent — most effectively spironolactone (PATHWAY-2 trial) — is added. Renal sympathetic denervation is an emerging interventional option for selected patients.

faqs.question: Is it safe to exercise with high blood pressure?
faqs.answer: Yes — regular aerobic exercise is one of the most effective treatments for hypertension and is safe for most people with controlled BP. 150 minutes per week of moderate-intensity activity (brisk walking, cycling, swimming) lowers systolic BP by 4-9 mm Hg. Before beginning vigorous exercise, ensure BP is controlled below 160/100 mm Hg. During exercise, monitor for symptoms (chest pain, severe shortness of breath, dizziness) and stop if these occur. Resistance training is also safe with moderate loads; avoid very heavy weightlifting, which can cause an acute BP spike. Breathing exercises (slow diaphragmatic breathing, 6 breaths per minute) independently lower BP by 3-5 mm Hg.

faqs.question: How much sodium per day is safe for someone with hypertension?
faqs.answer: The American Heart Association recommends under 2,300 mg of sodium per day for the general population and ideally under 1,500 mg per day for anyone with hypertension, stage-2 hypertension, or at high cardiovascular risk. The average American consumes 3,400 mg daily, with the largest sources being processed and restaurant foods — not table salt alone. Reading nutrition labels (sodium content per serving), cooking at home, limiting processed meats and canned soups, and choosing low-sodium bread and cereals are the most practical strategies. Reducing sodium by 1,000 mg per day lowers systolic BP by approximately 2-8 mm Hg.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ACE inhibitors and ARBs?
faqs.answer: Both ACE inhibitors and ARBs lower blood pressure by interrupting the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) — but at different points. ACE inhibitors block the angiotensin-converting enzyme that makes angiotensin II from angiotensin I; ARBs block the receptor (AT1) that angiotensin II acts on. The practical differences: ACE inhibitors cause a dry cough in 5-20% of patients (because ACE also breaks down bradykinin); ARBs do not. Both are equally effective for BP control and cardiovascular protection. ACE inhibitors are preferred after a heart attack or in heart failure with reduced EF; ARBs are preferred if a patient develops ACE-inhibitor cough. Never use both classes together — the combination increases kidney injury, hyperkalaemia, and hypotension without cardiovascular benefit.

faqs.question: Does alcohol affect blood pressure?
faqs.answer: Yes — alcohol has a dose-dependent relationship with blood pressure. Consuming more than 1-2 standard drinks per day raises BP chronically. Alcohol stimulates the sympathetic nervous system, increases cortisol release, and raises intracellular calcium (promoting vasoconstriction). Beyond 3-4 drinks per day, the effect is substantial and clinically significant. Alcohol also raises triglyceride levels, contributes to weight gain, and interacts with antihypertensive medications. The American Heart Association recommends <=2 drinks per day for men and <=1 per day for women. Reducing or eliminating alcohol in heavy drinkers can lower systolic BP by 4-8 mm Hg within weeks.

faqs.question: Can hypertension affect my eyes?
faqs.answer: Yes — the retinal circulation is a direct window into systemic microvascular health. Hypertensive retinopathy progresses through four grades: Grade I — mild narrowing of retinal arterioles; Grade II — more severe narrowing with arteriovenous nicking; Grade III — flame haemorrhages, cotton-wool spots, and hard exudates; Grade IV — papilloedema (swelling of the optic disc) plus all Grade III signs. Grades III-IV retinopathy indicate severely elevated BP with significant risk of end-organ damage in the brain and kidneys and warrant urgent treatment. Over time, chronic hypertension also contributes to retinal vein occlusions, retinal artery occlusions, and optic neuropathy. Annual fundoscopic examination is recommended for all hypertensives.

faqs.question: What is white-coat hypertension and does it need treatment?
faqs.answer: White-coat hypertension is blood pressure that is elevated in a clinical setting but normal outside of it — confirmed by home BP monitoring or ambulatory BP monitoring. It affects roughly 15-30% of people diagnosed with hypertension. While it does not represent sustained hypertension, it is not entirely benign: white-coat hypertensives have a higher cardiovascular risk than truly normotensive individuals (because the same risk factors that cause labile sympathetic activation also cause vascular dysfunction). They warrant lifestyle intervention (weight loss, exercise, salt restriction), annual monitoring with home BP checks, and may require medication if home BP monitoring subsequently shows progressive elevation.

faqs.question: What is the relationship between hypertension and kidney disease?
faqs.answer: The relationship between hypertension and chronic kidney disease (CKD) is bidirectional and self-reinforcing. Hypertension damages the small arteries within the kidneys, reducing the glomerular filtration rate (GFR) and causing ischaemic nephropathy. Falling GFR impairs the kidneys' ability to excrete sodium and water, activating the RAAS and raising BP further — a vicious cycle. By CKD stage 3 (eGFR 30-59), hypertension is present in over 80% of patients. Controlling BP aggressively (target <130/80, with ACE inhibitors or ARBs as first-line) is the single most important intervention to slow CKD progression and reduce cardiovascular risk in CKD patients.

faqs.question: How does hypertension cause stroke?
faqs.answer: Hypertension causes stroke through two main mechanisms. Ischaemic stroke: chronic high pressure damages the walls of small cerebral arteries, causing lipohyalinosis (fibrinoid necrosis and hyaline deposition in small penetrating arteries that supply the basal ganglia, thalamus, pons, and internal capsule). This leads to lacunar infarcts. Hypertension also accelerates atherosclerosis in the carotid and vertebral arteries, causing large-artery embolic strokes. Haemorrhagic stroke: chronic pressure overload causes small aneurysms (Charcot-Bouchard microaneurysms) in the penetrating arteries of the basal ganglia and thalamus; when these rupture, they cause intracerebral haemorrhage. Every 10 mm Hg rise in systolic BP roughly doubles the risk of intracerebral haemorrhage.

faqs.question: Is hypertension different in older adults?
faqs.answer: Yes — hypertension in older adults has a distinct physiology and requires modified management. In people over 65, the dominant mechanism shifts from high peripheral vascular resistance (young/middle-aged) to arterial stiffening — the aorta loses elasticity and cannot absorb systolic pressure waves, raising systolic BP while diastolic may fall (isolated systolic hypertension with wide pulse pressure). Orthostatic hypotension is common and worsens with over-treatment, increasing fall risk. The target systolic BP should be individualised: <130 is appropriate if well-tolerated and orthostatic hypotension is absent; <140 is acceptable for frail patients. Diastolic BP below 60 mm Hg in older adults on multiple medications warrants medication review, as reduced coronary perfusion during diastole becomes clinically significant with stiff coronaries.

faqs.question: Can I take ibuprofen or naproxen if I have hypertension?
faqs.answer: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (ibuprofen, naproxen, diclofenac) raise blood pressure by approximately 3-6 mm Hg, reduce the effectiveness of most antihypertensive drugs (particularly ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and diuretics), and increase cardiovascular risk when used chronically. They work by inhibiting renal prostaglandin synthesis, reducing sodium and water excretion, and increasing systemic vascular resistance. Occasional short-term use (a few days) for headache, fever, or acute pain is generally acceptable, but chronic daily NSAID use should be avoided in hypertensives. Paracetamol (acetaminophen) at standard doses has a much smaller effect on BP and is the preferred analgesic for regular use. If NSAIDs are necessary long-term (e.g., for arthritis), the lowest effective dose should be used with close BP monitoring.

symptomKeywords: when to worry about blood pressure
metaDescription: Hypertension (high blood pressure) affects 47% of US adults. Learn the 2017 ACC/AHA guidelines, drug options, DASH diet, and how to lower BP naturally.

reviewer.name: Dr. Sanjay Gupta
reviewer.bio: Dr. Gupta specialises in preventive cardiology, hypertension management, and cardiovascular risk reduction. He completed his cardiology fellowship at a major academic medical centre and has published on blood pressure control strategies in primary care.
reviewer.relevantExperience: Over 15 years in clinical cardiology; focus on resistant hypertension, cardiovascular prevention, and the interface between nephrology and cardiology.
reviewer.disclosure: No relevant financial conflicts of interest to disclose.
